text: Which testability aspect refers to the interfaces that give the test automation solution insight into the internal state and behaviour of the System Under Test?
explanation: Observability refers to the interfaces and mechanisms that allow the Test Automation Solution (TAS) to gain insight into the current state of the SUT, such as log outputs, APIs that expose state, and monitoring endpoints. Controllability refers to the ability to perform actions on the SUT. Architecture transparency relates to the clarity of component and interface documentation.

text: Which testability aspect of the SUT ensures that the Test Automation Solution can perform actions such as clicking UI elements, sending TCP/IP requests, or triggering USB events?
explanation: Controllability refers to the interfaces available to the TAS that allow it to perform actions on the SUT. Examples include UI controls (buttons, fields), TCP/IP sockets, USB interfaces, and API endpoints. Without sufficient controllability, automation cannot drive the SUT effectively.

text: A team wants to run automated component integration tests as part of their CI/CD pipeline. The tests should run without deploying the SUT to any server. Which test environment type is MOST appropriate for this purpose?
explanation: The build environment (also called the CI agent or build server) is where component tests and component integration tests are executed as part of the build process, without requiring full deployment of the SUT. It also includes static analysis. The integration environment is the first environment with full deployment and monitoring, and is used for system integration and acceptance testing.

text: Which deployment strategy in the production/operational environment involves directing a small percentage of real user traffic to a new version of the application while the majority of users continue using the old version?
explanation: A canary release involves gradually rolling out a new version to a small subset of users (the 'canary') while the rest continue using the stable version. This limits the blast radius of any defects in the new release. Blue/green deployment uses two identical environments and switches traffic between them. A/B testing compares two variants to measure user response.

text: During SUT analysis for test automation, a team discovers that certain third-party payment services are not available in the test environment and cannot be invoked during automated testing. What is the MOST appropriate solution?

options.text: Skip all tests that interact with the payment service.
Use emulation or simulation of the third-party system within the test environment.
Run those tests only in production using real payment transactions.
Redesign the SUT so that it no longer uses third-party payment services.

explanation: When third-party systems are unavailable in the test environment, the standard approach is to emulate or simulate those systems (using mocks, stubs, or service virtualisation). This is a key consideration in SUT analysis for automation planning. Skipping tests creates coverage gaps; running in production is dangerous; redesigning the SUT is not a testing responsibility.

text: Which of the following is a key consideration when analysing the SUT to determine WHICH test activities to automate?

options.text: The personal preference of the test manager regarding which tests look most interesting.
The availability of test data and the feasibility of emulating third-party dependencies.
The colour scheme of the test reporting dashboard.
The number of years the test team has been working together.

explanation: SUT analysis for automation includes evaluating the availability of test data and how third-party or external system dependencies can be managed (emulated, mocked, or stubbed). These factors directly determine which test activities are feasible to automate and at which test level. Other factors include identifying roles/skill sets needed and which test types to support.

text: When creating a tool evaluation comparison matrix, how should the evaluation be structured?

options.text: Tools are listed in rows and evaluation requirements are listed in columns.
Tools are listed in columns and evaluation requirements are listed in rows.
A single score is assigned to each tool without breaking it down by requirement.
Only the vendor's stated features are used; independent testing of tools is not recommended.

explanation: According to the ISTQB TAE syllabus, a tool evaluation comparison table places candidate tools in columns and the list of evaluation requirements (criteria) in rows. This structure allows each requirement to be assessed for each tool systematically. A single overall score without breakdown by requirement lacks the granularity needed for an informed decision.

text: Which of the following is a valid factor to consider when evaluating and comparing test automation tools?

options.text: The tool's ability to integrate with CI/CD pipelines and defect tracking systems.
The age of the tool's logo design.
The number of social media followers the tool vendor has.
Whether the tool's name is easy to pronounce in all team languages.

explanation: Tool evaluation factors include integration capabilities with CI/CD pipelines, defect tracking systems, test management tools, and version control. Other valid factors include the programming language/technology used, configuration ability, test data management support, reporting features, scalability, maintainability, and reliability. Marketing-related factors are not relevant to technical tool evaluation.

text: What type of test automation is mainly performed in the Preproduction environment?

options.text: Performance efficiency testing and user acceptance testing

explanation: Performance efficiency testing and user acceptance testing are mainly performed in the Preproduction environment because this environment is used to test real world scenarios. Component testing is performed in a build and development environment. Static analysis is usually done at the development environment. Component integration testing is done at the development environment.

text: In which environment is a fully automated test suite typically executed against a release candidate for the first time?
explanation: Robust user interface (UI) and application programming interface (API) test suites are typically executed against fully integrated systems in the Integration/test environment for the first time. Preproduction or staging environment testing has less focus on functional aspects. A build environment is not ready for more robust test execution. Full functional test suites are not typically executed against products in production.

text: You are working for an IT company which is developing a built-in Android-based car multimedia system. The software contains several components working together. Developers are following the test-driven development approach. After the development of the software, it is delivered to another IT company which integrates the software with the hardware elements and sells them together to car manufacturers. Which of the following should be considered during capturing the test automation requirements by the IT company you are working for?

options.text: Is it important for the test automation approach to support component testing?
Should the test automation approach support beta testing?
Is it important for the test automation approach to support the testing of the software in as many different types of cars as possible?
Which tester roles should be supported by the test automation approach?
Is it important for the test automation approach to support the mobile application store approval?

explanation: Component testing is performed by the developing IT company, so supporting it is a relevant automation requirement. Automated component tests are designed and executed by developers, so which tester roles should be supported is also a valid consideration. Beta testing is not performed by this company. Testing in many types of cars is performed by the integrator company. Mobile application store approval is not applicable to this company.

text: You are evaluating test automation tools. The following findings were recorded for one tool: it has a very informative dashboard; it includes a test logging component; it includes a customizable test reporting component; during the proof of concept, the tool performed very slowly against the SUT compared to other evaluated tools; the current test environment is valid according to the release notes. What should be your next step regarding the selection of this tool?

options.text: Acquire more hardware resources for the SUT to decrease the performance degradation.
Turn off test logging to improve performance of the tool.
Plan to migrate the SUT to another hardware/software environment where there is a possibility for the elimination of the tool overhead.

explanation: The tool does not meet selection requirements due to poor performance during the proof of concept. Since the test environment is already valid according to the release notes, acquiring more hardware for the SUT is not the right approach. Turning off test logging is not acceptable as the logging component is essential for troubleshooting. Migrating the SUT is completely wrong.

text: How does observability in the SUT help with test automation development?

options.text: The TAE can watch the code while it executes
The automation software can access interfaces that will let it determine if the expected results have been achieved
The automation software can access controls such as function calls, to drive the software and verify results
It makes the components and their interfaces more clear and easier to understand

explanation: Observability provides the automation software with access to interfaces (such as APIs, log outputs, and monitoring endpoints) that allow it to determine whether the expected results have been achieved. Option C describes controllability, not observability. Option D relates to architecture transparency.

text: Which environment should be used to run performance testing in order to obtain the most accurate results?
explanation: The staging (preproduction) environment should be used for performance testing as it is the closest replication of production. Performance tests can be run in all environments, but until everything is integrated in a production-representative system, the results may not be indicative of production performance.

text: Which environment is best suited for running automated API tests?
explanation: The integration environment is the best place to run automated API tests. They can be run earlier, but stubs and mocks will likely be required because the integrations are not in place. In the integration environment, all components are deployed and connected, enabling realistic API test execution.

text: You are working on a new test automation project. The developers have implemented a CI/CD pipeline with automatic generation of environments as needed. Everyone agrees that it would be best if the test automation code was executed automatically as the code passes through the pipeline. How does this affect your approach to the automation implementation?

options.text: You will need a tool that integrates easily and uses similar technology to the SUT
Environment setups will need to be tested prior to the execution of any test automation as they are being generated from code
There will be no need for a test architect since the developers have already implemented CI/CD and the automation code just needs to comply with the pipeline rules

explanation: In this environment, you will need a tool that allows you to develop code that will work with the SUT code — allowing automated execution within the CI/CD and simplifying the tool landscape. A may be true, but it is not specifically indicated by the question. C is less likely needed than if environments were manually configured. D is unlikely because test architects are needed with any complex automation solution.

text:
You have done the following tool comparison:

| Criteria | Tool A | Tool B | Tool C | Tool D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Record/Playback | O | O | O | X |
| Integrates CI/CD | O | P | P | O |
| API automation | O | X | P | O |
| Mobile capability | X | O | O | P |
| Source | COTS | COTS | Open Source | Open Source |

O = out of the box functionality, P = functionality can be implemented with programming, X = functionality is not available

You have assigned the following importance: Record/Playback - nice to have; Integrates with CI/CD - nice to have; API automation - must have; Mobile - nice to have; Source - open source preferred.

The team who will use this tool has strong programming skills.

Which is the best tool for your organization?
explanation: Tool D is the best choice. The team does not need record/playback capability since they are experienced programmers. Tool D is better than Tool C because it has built-in API testing capability (must have), is open source (preferred), and the team can implement mobile capability with their programming skills.

text: Which of the following information in API documentation is LEAST relevant for implementing automated tests on that API?

options.text: Release notes/change logs on past changes to the API.
Details about the parameters accepted by each API endpoint.
Authentication mechanisms required to access the API.
Details about the format of the API responses.

explanation: Implementing automated API tests requires precise, actionable interface specifications: endpoint parameters, authentication mechanisms, and response formats directly support test design (valid/invalid inputs, protected calls, robust assertions). Release notes and change logs are useful for understanding evolution and migration but are not typically required to implement tests for the API's current behaviour, making them the least relevant of the options.

text: A release candidate has passed all required functional tests after full integration. It is now necessary to perform reliability tests to evaluate whether the release can guarantee an MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) in production higher than a given threshold. Which test environment is BEST suited to perform these reliability tests?
explanation: Reliability testing (long-duration runs, endurance, MTBF measurement) requires an environment that closely resembles production in configuration, resource allocation, and operational characteristics, since such measurements are highly sensitive to environmental differences. A local dev environment is unrepresentative and unstable; a build environment focuses on packaging/fast verification; an integration environment is often shared, volatile, and may not match production sizing. Preproduction (staging) is the closest safe approximation to production while remaining controlled, making it the best fit for credible reliability evaluation.

text: Which of the following describes the LEAST relevant concern when selecting suitable test automation tools for a test automation project?

options.text: The degree of technical knowledge and skills within the test team to implement code-based test automation for the project.
For open-source tools, whether they are released under permissive or restrictive licenses, and whether they can be modified and by whom.
Whether the test team has been formed with the different personalities of its members in mind, to ensure effective interaction between them.
For commercial tools, what factors determine licensing costs, e.g., maximum number of users supported and whether the license is fixed or floating.

explanation: Tool selection focuses on factors that materially affect feasibility, cost of ownership, and sustainability: technical/skill fit (A), licensing constraints (B), and licensing cost models (D) are all directly relevant. Team personality mix, while important for general team effectiveness, does not describe tool capability, integration constraints, cost, or risk, and is typically a project/organisational concern rather than a tool-selection criterion.

text: In User Acceptance Testing (UAT) for a new SUT, in addition to the manual tests performed by end-users, automated tests are performed that focus on repetitive and routine test scenarios. In which of the following environments are these tests typically performed?
explanation: UAT is typically performed in an environment that is as production-like as feasible while still controlled and safe — the preproduction (staging) environment. It supports realistic end-to-end flows and lets business users validate acceptance criteria while enabling routine automated checks without risking live operations. A build environment focuses on compiling/packaging; an integration environment may not reflect full production-like configuration and is often shared/volatile; production is generally avoided for UAT due to business risk to live data and users.

text: You are evaluating a test automation tool for UI tests on a web app. The tests locate HTML elements, perform actions, and check expected text; they are independent and run nightly against the latest build. There is a high risk that the web app will crash while running some tests. Based only on this information, which is your MOST important concern when evaluating the tool?

options.text: Does the tool provide a feature to specify tests in a descriptive meta-language that is not directly executable on the web app?
Does the tool offer a feature to restore the web app, recover from the failed test, skip such tests, and resume the next one in the suite?
Does the tool offer a feature to create a mock server that simulates a real API by accepting requests and returning responses?
Does the tool support a licensing scheme that allows accessing different feature sets?

explanation: Given the explicit risk of the app crashing during unattended nightly runs, the highest-priority capability is resilience: recovering, continuing, and producing usable results despite a crash. If one crash aborts the whole suite, feedback is lost for many tests and triage cost rises. A descriptive meta-language (A) aids readability but is not the urgent need; a mock server (C) addresses dependency isolation, not the stated crash risk; licensing (D) affects procurement, not this operational risk.

text: Which aspect of 'design for testability' is MOST directly associated with the need to define precisely which interfaces are available in the SUT for test automation at different test levels?
explanation: Architecture transparency concerns how clearly a system's structure, layers, and accessible interfaces are documented and exposed, so automation can reliably connect to the right interaction points at each test level. Controllability is about the ability to set inputs, states, and preconditions; observability is about the ability to see outputs and internal state; autonomy concerns whether tests can run independently without external dependencies or manual intervention. The specific emphasis on precisely defining available interfaces across test levels is fundamentally an architecture transparency concern.
